QID11_1: Emotional Debt - I carry unresolved emotional strain from my work over time.
QID11_2: Emotional Debt - Emotional stress from previous work continues to affect me.
QID11_3: Emotional Debt - I keep working without addressing emotional difficulties related to work.
QID11_4: Emotional Debt - Accumulated frustration influences how I make work decisions.
QID11_5: Emotional Debt - Emotional strain does not disappear even when tasks are completed.
QID11_6: Emotional Debt - I feel emotionally “in debt” due to prolonged work pressure.
QID13_1: Emotional Smells - I worked late at night or during weekends repeatedly. (Persistent Off-Hours Activity)
QID13_2: Emotional Smells - I made many small commits instead of fewer well-structured ones. (Workload Compression)
QID13_3: Emotional Smells - I committed code very frequently within short time periods. (High-Frequency Commit Pattern)
QID13_4: Emotional Smells - My commit messages expressed frustration or negativity. (Negative Commit Sentiment)
QID13_5: Emotional Smells - I wrote commit messages with little or no explanation (e.g., “fix”, “again”). (Self-Referential Commits)
QID13_6: Emotional Smells - I avoided commenting, reviewing, or participating in discussions. (Communication Withdrawal)
QID13_7: Emotional Smells - I worked on the same files or modules mostly alone. (Lone Contribution Pattern)
QID13_8: Emotional Smells - I reworked the same code multiple times in a short period. (Repeated Rework)
QID13_9: Emotional Smells - I reverted recent changes repeatedly. (Repeated Reverts)
QID13_10: Emotional Smells - I applied fixes that did not fully solve the problem. (Non-Resolving Fixes)
QID13_11: Emotional Smells - I used AI-generated code with minimal review or adaptation. (Uncritical AI Code Adoption)

QID16: Talking with collegues of experience outside work. Explaining how I feel and the frustration in some tasks with senior collegues helps me a lot facing work stress.

Automate every repetitive and mind-numbing task;  Include tasks providing long-term value like writing documentation, robust code, maintanability etc. in everyday priorities;  Share persistent knowledge of the project among all team members so that everyone know what they are working on and what it is used for in the greater scheme;  Foster innovation, experiment new tools and methodologies, plan refactorings aimed at improving the maintanability of the code and discovering new, better ways to implement features.  Track every quirk and bug of the project and plan to fix them, even when the client knows nothing about them yet;  Ensure high observability by measuring the performances of the software product and its related infrastructure;    When things work well, every teammate is more satisfied and thus more productive. All the above suggestons help things work out sufficiently well even in the face of urgencies.

One practice that helped me manage stress at work was breaking tasks into small, prioritized steps and following a simple daily plan, which reduced overwhelm and gave me a clear sense of progress. I also took short walks or 5-minute breathing breaks between tasks to reset my focus and stay calm, which improved both my productivity and emotional balance.
